entry.title: Display CIM Approval Comment in S/4HANA Public Cloud Approval Workflow
entry.content:
Dear Partner Community,

we are currently working with Central Invoice Management (CIM) together with S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Our customer currently has two approval workflows in place:

  • the standard approval workflow in Central Invoice Management, and

  • the standard approval workflow in S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Our requirement is the following:
During the approval process in CIM, the approver should be able to enter a text/comment, and this text should then be visible in the approval workflow in S/4HANA Public Cloud.

At the moment, we are unsure about the best way to transfer or expose this text so that it is displayed during the approval step in S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Has anyone implemented a similar scenario or has ideas on how this could be handled (e.g., via extensibility, workflow context, or integration options)?

Any insights or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

Kind regards,
Philipp

entry.title: Re: Production Integration with Warehouse Management in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Hi @Matthias_G_ , sorry for the late reply.

This is controlled by the warehouse order creation rules - see here for documentation.

You could for example try to define a filter, leveraging the configuration option "Field to Limit Warehouse Order Size" to limit by the field "RDOCID Document ID". You then need to make sure you also sort the warehouse tasks accordingly. Please see the documentation of the configuration field itself for further information.

Best regards,
Christian

entry.title: Cloud ALM Documents – External File configuration missing (DMS not available in BTP entitlements)
entry.content:
Hello,

We are trying to use the Documents app in SAP Cloud ALM to upload files such as PowerPoint or PDF.

However, when opening a document and selecting the External File tab, we receive the message:

From the documentation, it appears that this requires SAP Document Management Service (DMS) to be configured via SAP BTP.

Our situation:

  • We have Global Admin access in SAP BTP

  • Our Cloud ALM tenant was not provisioned through BTP

  • The entitlement SAP Document Management Service does not appear in our BTP Global Account entitlements list

  • Therefore we cannot create a DMS instance to connect to Cloud ALM

Questions:

  1. Is Document Management Service required to upload documents (PowerPoint, PDF, etc.) in Cloud ALM?

  2. If the entitlement is not visible in BTP, does it need to be enabled by SAP for the global account?

  3. Is there an alternative repository configuration for document storage in Cloud ALM?

Any guidance would be appreciated.

Thanks.

entry.title: Streamlining Application Transport with SAP Cloud Transport Management on BTP
entry.content:
In today's multi-environment SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) landscape, managing application artifacts and content across Development, Test, and Production subaccounts can be challenging. SAP Cloud Transport Management (SAP CTM) provides a centralized, auditable solution for transporting deliverables across Cloud Foundry, ABAP, and Neo environments.

This article explores the capabilities of SAP CTM and provides a comprehensive, step­ by-step guide for implementing an end-to-end transport setup for SAP Build projects

What is SAP Cloud Transport Management?

SAP Cloud Transport Management is a centralized service within SAP BTP that enables organizations to:

  • Manage software deliverables across multiple BTP environments
  • Transport content between different subaccounts (Development, Test, Production)
  • Maintain comprehensive audit trails of all changes
  • Enable separation of concerns between development and operations teams

This separation allows developers to trigger transports from their development environment, while a central operations team manages imports into test and production environments, ensuring proper governance and control.

Supported Content Types

SAP CTM supports a wide range of content types across various BTP services and solutions. Understanding content types is crucial: a content type is an entity you select when creating a new node in SAP CTM. The following table highlights some of the most used services and their transportable content:

SAP BTP Service/ SAP Cloud Solution

Content

Content Type

Environment

Content Agent Service

Joule Studio in SAP Build

Joule Studio projects

MTA

Cloud Foundry

Yes (for import)

SAP Analytics Cloud

Content network packages

Application Content

Cloud Foundry / Neo

No

SAP API Management

API artifacts

MTA

Cloud Foundry

Yes (for content selection and deployment)

SAP Asset Performance Management

Communication system configurations

Coding masks

Risk and criticality assessment templates

Strategy assessment
templates

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP Batch Release Hub for Life Sciences

Configuration settings

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP BTP ABAP environment

References to SAP BTP ABAP environment content

BTP ABAP

SAP BTP ABAP environment

n/a

SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Runtime

HTML5 apps/ Java apps/ Node Js apps

MTA

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP BTP, Neo application runtime

Destinations/ HTML 5 roles/ HTML5 apps/ Java apps/ SAP Fiori apps/ SAP Fiori Portal/ SAP Fiori roles/ Authorization groups

MTA

Neo

n/a

SAP Build Apps (part of SAP Build)

Authorization groups

MTA

Neo

n/a

SAP Build Apps (part of SAP Build)

Projects

MTA

Cloud Foundry

Yes (for export and import)

SAP Build Process Automation

Projects

MTA

Cloud Foundry

Yes (for export and import)

SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition

Content

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

Content

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP Datasphere

Content packages

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP Destination Service

Destinations

MTA

Cloud Foundry

Yes

SAP Digital Manufacturing

Production process designs

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP Entitlement Management

Configuration content

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP Excise Tax Management

Warehouses

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP Group Reporting Data Collection

Forms and folders

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP HANA Deployment Infrastructure (HDI)

HDI content

MTA

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP HANA XS classic model

Delivery units (DU)

XSC DU

Neo

n/a

SAP Integration Suite

Content

MTA

Neo

n/a

SAP Integration Suite

Integration packages (integration, flows, value mappings, APIs)

MTA

Cloud Foundry

Yes (for content selection and deployment)

SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management

Configuration settings

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP Mobile Services

Application configurations

MTA

 

Yes

SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model

Environments

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

SAP Risk and Assurance Management

In-house business content (controls, automated procedures, manual procedures and regulations)

Application Content

Cloud Foundry

No

Note: Multitarget Application Archives (MTAs) are packages containing cloud applications or content created within cloud applications. For example, SAP Cloud Integration content is packaged and transported as an MTA.

Transport Architecture: Three-Subaccount Model

The recommended architecture for SAP CTM implementation involves four distinct subaccounts [Dev, Test, Prod and Central], each serving a specific purpose:

Source Subaccount (Development)

  • Primary development environment where SAP Build Apps projects are created and edited
  • Developers have full editing capabilities

Target Subaccount (Test/Production)

  • Destination environment for transported content
  • SAP Build Apps projects are view-only in these tenants, ensuring controlled deployment

Central Subaccount (Transport Management)

  • Administrative hub for managing content transport between Source and Target tenants
    Fig: Content Flow Architecture

Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

This section provides a detailed walkthrough for setting up SAP Cloud Transport Management to transport SAP Build projects from Development to Test environments.

Prerequisites

Source Subaccount (Development):

  • Enable Cloud Foundry Environment
  • Subscribe to SAP Build Apps
  • Subscribe to Content Agent Service
  • Create an instance of Content Agent Service
  • Generate a service key for the Content Agent instance
    Fig: Check Cloud Foundry Subscription

Fig: Check Content Agent Service and SAP Build Process Automation Subscription

Fig: Check Service Key in Development System

Target Subaccount (Test):

  • Enable Cloud Foundry environment
  • Subscribe to SAP Build Apps
  • Subscribe to Content Agent Service
  • Create an instance of Content Agent Service
  • Generate a service key for the Content Agent instance

Central Subaccount:

  • Subscribe to Cloud Transport Management Service (Documentation),
  • Create an instance of Cloud Transport Management (Documentation),
  • Generate or obtain a service key from an administrator (Documentation)

Fig: Subscribe to Cloud Transport Management and Create Service Key

Configuring BTP Destinations

Step 1: Configure TransportManagementService Destination (Source Subaccount)

In your Development (Source) subaccount, create a destination with the following configuration (Documentation)

Property

Value

Name

TransportManagementService (case sensitive)

Type

HTTP

Description

mention relevant description

URL

uri from CTMS service key details

Proxy Type

Internet

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

uaa → clientid from CTMS service key details

Client Secret

uaa → clientsecret from CTMS service key details

Token Service URL Type

Dedicated

Token Service URL

uaa → url from CTMS service key details + /oauth/token

Additional properties

sourceSystemId = DEV (this will be the name of Source node in CTMS)
sourceSystemId.sap.build = DEV

Fig: Mention the highlighted fields in the Service Key creation

Verification: Navigate to the Content Agent user interface in your Source tenant. A green "Connected" status in the Cloud Transport Management tile confirms successful configuration.

Fig: Check Connection Status

Step 2: Configure Content Agent Destinations (Central Subaccount)

In your Central subaccount, create destinations pointing to each Target tenant (Test, Production) [Documentation]:

Property

Value

Name

Pick names that have the tenant role in them, for example BUILD_TEST and BUILD_PROD

Type

HTTP

Description

 

URL

url from CAS service key details

Proxy Type

Internet

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

uaa → clientid from CAS service key details

Client Secret

uaa → clientsecret from CAS service key details

Token Service URL Type

Dedicated

Token Service URL

uaa → url from CAS service key details + /oauth/token

 

Setting Up the Transport Landscape

Access the Cloud Transport Management application in your Central subaccount to configure the transport landscape.

Step 1: Create Source Node (DEV)

  • Open Cloud Transport Management application
  • Navigate to Transport Nodes
  • Create a new node with name "DEV"
  • Select appropriate content type (e.g., MTA for SAP Build Apps)

Fig: Transport node in development subaccount

Step 2: Create Target Node (TEST)

  • Create another node with name "TEST"
  • Configure with matching content type

Fig: Transport Node in test Subaccount

Step 3: Create Transport Route

  • Navigate to Transport Routes
  • Create a new route from "DEV" to "TEST"
  • Activate the route

Fig: Transport Route from Development to Test

Executing the Transport

Step 1: Initiate Transport from Development

  • Open SAP Build Process Automation application in your development tenant
  • Select the project version you want to transport
  • Click the Transport button to create a transport request

Fig: Transport the project from Development to Test i n SAP Build

Step 2: Review of Cloud Transport Management

  • Open Cloud Transport Management application in Central tenant
  • Verify the transport request appears in the queue

Important Note: If the "Transport Operator" role is not assigned to your user in the Central tenant, the Import option will be disabled. Ensure the "Import Operator" role is properly assigned before proceeding.

Fig: Check if the Import option is reflecting in CTMS

Step 3: Import Transport Request

  • Select the transport request
  • Click Import to deploy to the TEST node
  • Monitor the import progress

Fig: Check Status of import

 

Step 4: Verify in Target Environment

  • Open SAP Build Apps in your Test tenant
  • Check the import queue to confirm the transported content
    Fig: Check import queue in SAP Build

Step 5: Verify Import Completion

  •  After successful import, open SAP Build Apps in the Test tenant
  •  Verify that the project is now available and visible
  •  Confirm the project is in view-only mode (as expected for target environments

Fig: Import Project in SAP Build

Tip: You can configure additional transport routes following the same pattern to move content from Test to Production environments

Troubleshooting Common Issues

Issue 1: Connection Not Showing as "Connected" in Content Agent

Solution: Verify that all credentials in the TransportManagementService destination are correctly copied from the CTMS service key. Pay special attention to the Token Service URL format.

Issue 2: Import Option Disabled in CTMS

Solution: Ensure the "Import Operator" role is assigned to your user in the Central subaccount. Without this role, you'll only be able to view transport requests but not import them.

 

Issue 3: Transport Request Not Appearing in CTMS

Solution: Check that the sourcesystemld in the TransportManagementService destination matches exactly with the node name created in CTMS. This is case­ sensitive.


Conclusion

SAP Cloud Transport Management provides a robust, centralized solution for managing application lifecycle across SAP BTP environments. By implementing the three­ subaccount architecture and following the configuration steps outlined in this guide, organizations can establish a controlled, auditable transport process that ensures quality and governance throughout the development lifecycle.

The separation of concerns between development and operations teams, combined with comprehensive audit trails, makes SAP CTM an essential tool for enterprises managing complex SAP BTP landscapes. Whether you're working with SAP Build Apps, SAP Build Process Automation, Integration Suite, or any of the supported content types, SAP CTM streamlines your deployment process while maintaining the control and visibility needed for production environments.

By following this guide, you now have a complete understanding of how to set up and execute transports between Development, Test, and Production environments, enabling efficient and governed application delivery across your SAP BTP landscape.

entry.title: Re: Missing Depreciation Area in Asset Master despite Com...
entry.content:
Good day @Wayne10 

Thank you for your question.

What you described is a Public Cloud restriction. A depreciation area can be configured at company code level, but it will not appear in the asset master if you do not assign the related valuation view or it is inactive for the asset class. See KBA 3696205 for more details. In other words:

  • Company code configuration defines what is technically available.
  • Asset master display depends on which valuation views that are active for the asset class, so inactive valuation views hide their depreciation areas.

Here is what I suggest:

  • In the Accounting Principle Assignment to Ledger and Company Code activity, verify that the ledger group 2L is actually assigned to US company code. See KBA 3696205 for more details.

If 2L is not assigned, then valuation views for 2L will not be available for activation in the asset class, and depreciation areas that are tied to that valuation view will not be visible.

  • In SSCUI 102609, open asset class 1100 for US compan code, check if there is a valuation view that corresponds to the ledger group behind Area 16. If it is missing or inactive, activate or add it according to what the activity allows in your tenant.
  • After activating the valuation view for the asset class, create a new test asset and check Valuation again. The reason is, if the area was inactive at the time, you create it, older assets may not show it the same way, depending on the scenario and the specific valuation view setup.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: WSSE error "The security token could not be authenticated or authorized" in Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Hello All,
I am currently working on an integration flow where SOAP 1.x adapter with WS-Security configuration is used in combination with a request-reply step. The request execution completes successfully but after the response generation phase the request-reply step fails with the following error - "org.apache.cxf.binding.soap.SoapFault: The security token could not be authenticated or authorized, cause: org.apache.wss4j.common.ext.WSSecurityException: The security token could not be authenticated or authorized"

I would like to understand if some kind of validation is occurring for the response payload at the SAP CPI side and is that why it is failing and that is there any mechanism to bypass this validation? Also would like to get clarity on another thing - in the response payload in the binary security token, valuetype attribute is PKIPathv1, which means the complete certificate chain is bundled together for signing purpose. But in SAP cpi we have uploaded the certificates separately as leaf, intermediate and root. Can this be a cause? 


When trace is enabled, I am able to see the SOAP response payload in the message logs. However, I would like to understand how the response payload can be captured within the exception subprocess when this failure occurs. I tried using ${in.body}, but it provided a blank body.
Has anyone encountered a similar scenario or have suggestions on how to capture the response payload in the exception subprocess?
Kindly see the below attachments for reference.

entry.title: Hello SAP Community
entry.content:
I'm Purnima from Guntur,

I’m currently working on SAP FICO IMPLEMENTATION PROJECT where I am gaining hands-on experience in Financial Accounting and Controlling processes. Through this project, I am learning how real business requirements are analyzed and configured in SAP, including areas like General Ledger, Accounts Payable, Accounts Receivable, and Asset Accounting.

This experience is helping me understand how organizations manage their financial transactions, reporting, and internal controls using SAP systems.

I’m passionate about continuously improving my knowledge in SAP and understanding how technology supports financial decision-making in organizations.

entry.title: Issues in THR81 system based accessment (Successfactors Employee Central)
entry.content:
Hello,

I completed the assessment today but encountered two technical issues that prevented me from finishing certain tasks.

1. While trying to export the Country Specific Corporate Data Model, the system kept showing errors. I also tried exporting the XML file from Provisioning, but the export was unsuccessful.

2. In the Business Rule Engine, the search functionality was not working and the option to configure rules using previous values was not available. Since this is required for the task, I was unable to complete that part.

I successfully completed the remaining tasks, but the result shows a score of 50% and a failed status. I believe the technical issues may have affected the scoring and I attempted all the others so there is less to no chance for fail.

I have completed 7 out of 9 tasks successfully and those 2 becauses of these errors I was able to complete it partially only.
what to do now?

entry.title: Use list of values from another version to target version in SAC data action
entry.content:
Hello All,

I have a requirement where i need to run a data action advance formula script on Target version based on the values from another version.

For Ex:

Version 1 is my source version from where I want list of values for DIMENSION 1 and Version 2 is my target version on which I am running the data action.

Version 1:

DIM1DIM2DIM3Measure
KR112DI112MP1129
KR113DI113MP1138
KR114DI114MP11410

 

Version 2:

DIM1DIM2DIM3Measure
KR112DI112MP1122
KR113DI113MP1133
KR114DI114MP1144
KR115DI115MP1155
KR116DI116MP1166
KR117DI117MP1177

I want to run a data action only on those value which is available in Version 1 for DIMENSION1. So let's say i want all values multiply by -1, then my desired outcome should be like this:

 

DIM1DIM2DIM3Measure
KR112DI112MP112-2
KR113DI113MP113-3
KR114DI114MP114-4
KR115DI115MP1155
KR116DI116MP1166
KR117DI117MP1177

Basically i would like to understand, how we can use the dimension members which is available in another version. Is that possible?

@N1kh1l @james_lim @William_Yu1 

Thanks

entry.title: Knowledge Graphs on SAP HANA: From Zero to Enterprise RAG with Triple Store
entry.content:
Introduction

If you’ve ever worked with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, you know the drill, you embed your documents into vectors, store them in a vector database, and at query time, you retrieve the top-K most similar chunks to feed as context to an LLM. It works. But there’s a problem.

Vector search alone is semantically “flat.” It tells you which chunks look similar to a query, but it doesn’t understand the relationships between entities in your data. It cannot reason about connections, follow chains of facts, or understand how entity A relates to entity B through entity C.

This is where Knowledge Graphs come in.

In this blog, I’ll walk you through — from absolute scratch — how i built a Knowledge Graph layer on SAP HANA using a custom Triple Store, and how i combined it with vector embedding-based retrieval to create a Hybrid RAG system that gives our AI agents both relevance (from vectors) and reasoning (from graphs).

Everything you see is from POC codebase. Let’s begin.

Table of Contents

  1. What is a Knowledge Graph?
  2. What are RDF Triplets?
  3. Why Do We Need Knowledge Graphs in RAG?
  4. Extracting Triplets from Text
    • 4a. Rule-Based Extraction with spaCy
    • 4b. LLM-Based Extraction
  5. The Multi-Agent Triplet Processing Pipeline
  6. Storing Triplets in SAP HANA Triple Store
  7. The Complete Ingestion Pipeline
  8. Querying the Knowledge Graph
  9. Hybrid Retrieval: Vector + Graph
  10. The Full RAG Pipeline
  11. Exposing via MCP for AI Agents
  12. Architecture Summary
  13. Conclusion

1. What is a Knowledge Graph?

A Knowledge Graph is a structured representation of real-world entities and the relationships between them. Think of it as a web of connected facts.

For example, consider this sentence:

“SAP SE is headquartered in Walldorf, Germany and develops enterprise software.”

A human reading this immediately understands three facts: - SAP SE → is_headquartered_in → Walldorf, Germany - SAP SE → develops → enterprise software - Walldorf → is_located_in → Germany

A Knowledge Graph captures exactly these facts as structured, queryable data. Each fact is stored as a triplet (more on this next).

Why is this different from a regular database?

Feature Relational Database Knowledge Graph

StructureFixed schema (tables, columns)Flexible (entities + relationships)
RelationshipsJOINs across tablesFirst-class citizens (edges)
Schema changesALTER TABLE neededJust add new triplets
Query styleSQL with JOINsGraph traversal / pattern matching
Best forStructured, predictable dataConnected, evolving knowledge

A Knowledge Graph is schema-flexible — you don’t need to define all possible relationships upfront. You can keep adding new types of facts without changing any table structure.

2. What are RDF Triplets?

RDF stands for Resource Description Framework. It’s a standard model for representing knowledge as triplets — the atomic unit of a Knowledge Graph.

Every triplet has three parts:

(Subject, Predicate, Object)
  • Subject: The entity we’re talking about (e.g., “SAP SE”)
  • Predicate: The relationship or property (e.g., “is_headquartered_in”)
  • Object: The value or target entity (e.g., “Walldorf”)

Visual Representation

    [SAP SE] ---is_headquartered_in--→ [Walldorf]
       |                                    |
       |---develops--→ [Enterprise Software] |
                                            |
                       [Germany] ←--is_located_in---┘

Each arrow is a triplet. A collection of triplets forms a graph — hence “Knowledge Graph.”

Why Triplets?

Triplets are powerful because they are: 1. Atomic — Each triplet is a self-contained fact 2. Composable — You can combine millions of triplets into a unified graph 3. Queryable — You can traverse relationships: “What does SAP SE develop?” or “Where is the company headquartered?” 4. Language-agnostic — The structure is universal regardless of source language

3. Why Do We Need Knowledge Graphs in RAG?

Let’s understand this with a concrete example.

The Problem with Vector-Only RAG

Imagine your enterprise has documents about employees, projects, and departments. A user asks:

“Who is the project lead for Project Phoenix and which department does that person belong to?”

With vector-only RAG, the system: 1. Embeds the query into a vector 2. Finds the top-K most similar document chunks 3. Hopes that one single chunk contains all the information

But what if: - Chunk A says: “John Smith is the project lead for Project Phoenix” - Chunk B says: “John Smith works in the Cloud Engineering department”

Vector search might retrieve Chunk A (it’s most similar to the query), but miss Chunk B entirely. The LLM then cannot answer the full question.

How Knowledge Graphs Solve This

With a Knowledge Graph, we have these triplets stored:

(John Smith, is_project_lead_of, Project Phoenix)
(John Smith, works_in, Cloud Engineering)
(Cloud Engineering, is_department_of, SAP)

When the system retrieves Chunk A via vector search and finds “John Smith,” it can traverse the graph to discover that John Smith works in Cloud Engineering — even though that second fact was in a completely different document chunk.

The Power of Hybrid Retrieval

Capability Vector Search Knowledge Graph Hybrid (Both)

Semantic similarity✅❌✅
Relationship traversal❌✅✅
Multi-hop reasoning❌✅✅
Handles unseen phrasing✅❌✅
Entity connections❌✅✅

By combining both, we get the best of both worlds: semantic understanding from vectors AND structured reasoning from graphs.

4. Extracting Triplets from Text

The first step in building a Knowledge Graph is extracting triplets from unstructured text. i implemented two approaches in our system.

4a. Rule-Based Extraction with spaCy (NLP Approach)

Our first approach uses spaCy, a natural language processing library, to extract Subject-Verb-Object (SVO) patterns from sentences.

Here’s the code from our triplets_service.py:

import re
from typing import List, Tuple
import spacy

_nlp = None

def get_nlp():
    global _nlp
    if _nlp is None:
        _nlp = spacy.load("en_core_web_sm")
    return _nlp

def clean(text: str) -> str:
    """Basic text cleaning function"""
    return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).strip()

def extract_svo_spacy(sentence: str):
    nlp = get_nlp()
    doc = nlp(sentence)
    triplets = []
    for token in doc:
        if token.pos_ == "VERB":  # processing every verb
            # subject can be to the left or as a child
            subj = [w for w in token.children if w.dep_ in ("nsubj", "nsubjpass")]
            objs = [w for w in token.children if w.dep_ in ("dobj", "attr", "dative", "oprd")]

            # prepositional objects
            for prep in [w for w in token.children if w.dep_ == "prep"]:
                pobj = [w for w in prep.children if w.dep_ == "pobj"]
                objs.extend(pobj)

            if subj and objs:
                for o in objs:
                    triplets.append((clean(subj[0].text), clean(token.lemma_), clean(o.text)))
    return triplets

How This Works — Step by Step

  1. Load the spaCy model (en_core_web_sm) — a pre-trained English NLP model
  2. Parse the sentence — spaCy tokenizes it and identifies parts of speech (POS) and dependency relations
  3. Find every VERB — verbs are potential predicates in our triplets
  4. Find subjects — tokens with dependency labels nsubj (nominal subject) or nsubjpass (passive nominal subject)
  5. Find objects — tokens with dependency labels dobj (direct object), attr (attribute), etc.
  6. Handle prepositional objects — “works in engineering” → the object is “engineering” via the preposition “in”
  7. Build triplets — (subject_text, verb_lemma, object_text)

Example:
Input: "SAP develops enterprise software"
Output: [("SAP", "develop", "software")]

Batch Processing

i also have a batch function in triplets_service.py to process an entire corpus:

def extract_corpus_triplets(corpus: List[str]) -> List[List[Tuple[str, str, str]]]:
    """extracting triplets from a list of text chunks"""
    results = []
    for text in corpus:
        sent = text.strip()
        if not sent:
            continue
        triplets = extract_svo_spacy(sent)
        results.append(triplets)
    return results

Limitations of Rule-Based Extraction

While fast and deterministic, spaCy-based extraction has limitations: - Misses implicit relationships (“Headquartered in Walldorf since 1972” — the subject “SAP” isn’t in this fragment) - Produces shallow predicates (just the verb lemma, not semantic relationship names) - Struggles with complex sentences and nested clauses

This is why i built a second, more powerful approach.

4b. LLM-Based Extraction (AI-Powered Approach)

Our primary extraction method uses a Large Language Model (via SAP AI Core) to understand text semantically and produce high-quality triplets.

Here’s the code from our knowledge_graph_service.py:

async def generate_triplets(text_chunk: str):
    """
    Uses an LLM via the Generative AI Hub SDK to extract RDF triplets
    from a given text chunk.
    """
    prompt_for_triplets = f"""
    Extract key factual statements from the text as RDF triplets.

    STRICT REQUIREMENTS:
    1. Return ONLY a valid JSON object
    2. Use exactly this format: {{"triplets": [["subject", "predicate", "object"]]}}
    3. Each triplet must have exactly 3 elements
    4. Use clear, concise predicates (e.g., "is", "has", "uses", "located_in")
    5. Maximum 15 triplets per response
    6. No explanations, no extra text outside JSON

    Text: "{text_chunk}"

    JSON:
    """

    try:
        response_content = await get_llm_response_async(prompt=prompt_for_triplets)
        response_json = json.loads(response_content)
        triplets = response_json.get("triplets", [])
        # converting the inner lists to tuples for consistency
        return [tuple(triplet) for triplet in triplets]

    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        print("Failed to parse JSON response from LLM.")
        return []
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"An error occurred: {e}")
        return []

How This Works

  1. Craft a precise prompt — We instruct the LLM to extract facts as [subject, predicate, object] arrays in JSON format
  2. Call the LLM asynchronously — via get_llm_response_async() which hits SAP AI Core’s inference API
  3. Parse the JSON response — extract the triplets array
  4. Convert to tuples — for consistency with the rest of our pipeline
  5. Handle errors gracefully — JSON parse failures and API errors return empty lists

Why LLM-Based Extraction is Superior

Aspect spaCy (Rule-Based) LLM (AI-Powered)

Implicit relationships❌Misses them✅Infers from context
Semantic predicates❌Raw verb lemmas✅Meaningful labels like “is_headquartered_in”
Complex sentences❌Often fails✅Handles well
Consistency✅Deterministic⚠️May vary (hence we validate)
Speed✅Very fast⚠️API call overhead
Cost✅Free⚠️LLM API costs

Batch Processing with Async Concurrency

Processing an entire corpus (potentially hundreds of chunks) sequentially would be extremely slow. i use asyncio.gather for concurrent processing:

async def convert_corpus_to_triplets_async(
    corpus: list,
    use_orchestrator: bool = True,
) -> List[List[Tuple[str, str, str]]]:
    """Convert entire corpus to triplets using async concurrency"""
    if not corpus:
        return []

    try:
        if use_orchestrator:
            orchestrator = TripletOrchestrator()
            results = await orchestrator.process_corpus(corpus)
            return results
        else:
            # Run all LLM calls concurrently
            tasks = [generate_triplets(text) for text in corpus]
            results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

            processed_results = []
            for i, result in enumerate(results):
                if isinstance(result, Exception):
                    print(f"Error processing chunk {i}: {result}")
                    processed_results.append([])
                else:
                    processed_results.append(result)
            return processed_results

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error in convert_corpus_to_triplets_async: {e}")
        return [[]] * len(corpus)

Notice the use_orchestrator flag — when True, i use our Multi-Agent Pipeline for higher quality triplets. Let’s dive into that next.

5. The Multi-Agent Triplet Processing Pipeline

Raw LLM output isn’t always perfect. The model might produce inconsistent predicate names, duplicate triplets, or factually questionable relationships. To solve this, i built a multi-agent processing pipeline where specialized agents collaborate to refine triplets.

Architecture Overview

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   TripletOrchestrator                     │
│                                                          │
│  ┌────────────┐   ┌───────────┐   ┌───────────┐        │
│  │  Analyzer   │──→│  Semantic  │──→│ Validator  │        │
│  │   Agent     │   │  Cleaner   │   │   Agent    │        │
│  └────────────┘   └───────────┘   └───────────┘        │
│        │                                    │            │
│        │          ┌───────────┐              │            │
│        └─error──→ │   JSON     │              │            │
│                   │  Repair    │              │            │
│                   └───────────┘              │            │
│                                    ┌────────▼────────┐  │
│                                    │   Aggregator     │  │
│                                    │     Agent        │  │
│                                    └─────────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

The State Object

All agents share a common state object defined in state_schema.py:

class TripletState(BaseModel):
    """State management for triplet processing pipeline"""
    raw_text: str
    initial_triplets: List[List[str]] = []
    cleaned_triplets: List[List[str]] = []
    validated_triplets: List[List[str]] = []
    final_triplets: List[List[str]] = []

    # metadata and tracking
    processing_state: str = "initial"
    error_messages: List[str] = []
    quality_scores: Dict[str, float] = {}
    retry_count: int = 0
    max_retries: int = 3

    # agent outputs
    analyzer_feedback: Optional[str] = None
    cleaner_feedback: Optional[str] = None
    validator_feedback: Optional[str] = None

This is a shared state pattern — each agent reads from and writes to specific fields, and the orchestrator controls the flow.

Stage 1: Analyzer Agent

The AnalyzerAgent in analyzer_node.py performs the initial extraction with a quality assessment:

class AnalyzerAgent:
    """Analyzes text and extracts initial triplets with quality assessment"""

    async def process(self, state: TripletState) -> TripletState:
        try:
            prompt = self._create_analysis_prompt(state.raw_text)
            response = await get_llm_response_async(prompt)
            analysis_result = self._parse_response(response)

            if analysis_result["success"]:
                state.initial_triplets = analysis_result["triplets"]
                state.quality_scores["analyzer"] = analysis_result["quality_score"]
                state.analyzer_feedback = analysis_result["feedback"]
                state.processing_state = "analyzed"
            else:
                state.error_messages.append(f"Analyzer failed: {analysis_result['error']}")
        except Exception as e:
            state.error_messages.append(f"Analyzer exception: {str(e)}")
        return state

The analyzer’s prompt asks the LLM to not only extract triplets but also rate the quality (0-1 score) and provide feedback about text complexity. This metadata helps downstream agents.

Stage 2: Semantic Cleaner Agent

The SemanticCleanerAgent in semantic_cleaner_agent.py standardizes the triplets:

class SemanticCleanerAgent:
    """Cleans and standardizes triplets for consistency"""

    def _create_cleaning_prompt(self, triplets: List[List[str]]) -> str:
        triplets_str = json.dumps(triplets)
        return f"""
        Clean and standardize the following triplets for better semantic consistency.

        CLEANING TASKS:
        1. Normalize entity names (remove extra spaces, fix capitalization)
        2. Standardize predicates (use consistent naming like "is_a", "has_property", "located_in")
        3. Remove duplicate or very similar triplets
        4. Fix obvious semantic issues
        5. Ensure subjects and objects are meaningful entities

        REQUIREMENTS:
        - Maintain factual accuracy
        - Use snake_case for predicates
        - Remove triplets with generic or meaningless entities

        Original triplets: {triplets_str}
        """

This agent ensures that predicates like "is located in", "located_in", and "is_in" all become a consistent "located_in". This consistency is critical for reliable graph queries later.

Stage 3: Validator Agent

The TripletValidatorAgent in triplet_validator_agent.py cross-checks triplets against the original text:

class TripletValidatorAgent:
    """Validates triplets for factual accuracy and relevance"""

    def _create_validation_prompt(self, triplets, original_text):
        return f"""
        Validate the following triplets against the original text for factual accuracy.

        VALIDATION CRITERIA:
        1. Factual accuracy: Are the relationships stated correctly?
        2. Semantic validity: Do the predicates make sense?
        3. Completeness: Are important facts missing?
        4. Consistency: Are there contradictions?
        5. Relevance: Are all triplets relevant to the source text?

        Original text: "{original_text}"
        Triplets to validate: {json.dumps(triplets)}
        """

This is a crucial fact-checking step — the validator has access to both the triplets AND the original text, so it can verify that no hallucinated facts slipped through.

Stage 4: Aggregator Agent

The AggregatorAgent in aggregator_node.py selects the best output and calculates an overall quality score:

class AggregatorAgent:
    """Aggregates results from all agents and determines final output"""

    def _select_best_triplets(self, state: TripletState) -> List[List[str]]:
        """Select the best quality triplets from processing pipeline"""
        if state.validated_triplets:
            return state.validated_triplets
        elif state.cleaned_triplets:
            return state.cleaned_triplets
        elif state.initial_triplets:
            return state.initial_triplets
        else:
            return []

    def _calculate_overall_quality(self, state: TripletState) -> float:
        """Weighted average quality score"""
        weights = {
            "analyzer": 0.3,
            "cleaner": 0.3,
            "validator": 0.4   # validator has higher weight
        }
        weighted_sum = 0.0
        total_weight = 0.0
        for agent, weight in weights.items():
            if agent in state.quality_scores:
                weighted_sum += state.quality_scores[agent] * weight
                total_weight += weight
        return weighted_sum / total_weight if total_weight > 0 else 0.0

Notice the graceful degradation — if validation fails, it falls back to cleaned triplets; if cleaning fails, it uses the initial triplets. The system never completely fails.

The Orchestrator

The TripletOrchestrator in orchestrator.py wires everything together with retry logic:

class TripletOrchestrator:
    def __init__(self):
        self.analyzer = AnalyzerAgent()
        self.cleaner = SemanticCleanerAgent()
        self.validator = TripletValidatorAgent()
        self.aggregator = AggregatorAgent()
        self.json_repair = JSONRepairAgent()

    async def preprocess_text_chunk(self, text_chunk: str) -> List[Tuple[str, str, str]]:
        state = TripletState(raw_text=text_chunk)
        try:
            # Stage 1: Initial analysis (with retries)
            while True:
                state = await self.analyzer.process(state)
                if not self._should_retry(state):
                    break
                state.retry_count += 1

            # Stage 2: Semantic cleaning
            if state.initial_triplets and not self._has_critical_errors(state):
                while True:
                    state = await self.cleaner.process(state)
                    if not self._should_retry(state):
                        break
                    state.retry_count += 1

            # Stage 3: Validation
            if (state.cleaned_triplets or state.initial_triplets) and not self._has_critical_errors(state):
                while True:
                    state = await self.validator.process(state)
                    if not self._should_retry(state):
                        break
                    state.retry_count += 1

            # Stage 4: Aggregation (no retries needed)
            state = self.aggregator.process(state)
            return [tuple(triplet) for triplet in state.final_triplets]

        except Exception as e:
            return []

Each stage has retry logic — if an agent fails (e.g., LLM returns malformed JSON), the orchestrator retries up to max_retries (3) times before moving to the next stage.

6. Storing Triplets in SAP HANA Triple Store

Now that we can extract high-quality triplets, we need somewhere to store them. We built a Triple Store as a column table in SAP HANA.

Table Schema

From our hana_repository.py, here’s the table creation:

def ensure_triple_store():
    """Create TRIPLE STORE table and indexes if they don't exist"""
    conn = get_hana_db()
    try:
        # Check if table exists
        exists_sql = """
        SELECT 1 FROM SYS.TABLES
        WHERE TABLE_NAME = 'TRIPLE_STORE'
        AND SCHEMA_NAME = CURRENT_SCHEMA
        """
        result = conn.sql(exists_sql).collect()
        if not result.empty:
            return

        # Create the table
        create_sql = """
        CREATE COLUMN TABLE TRIPLE_STORE (
            ID BIGINT GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
            EMB_REF_ID NVARCHAR(36) NOT NULL,
            CHUNK_INDEX INTEGER,
            SUBJECT NVARCHAR(500),
            PREDICATE NVARCHAR(200),
            OBJECT NVARCHAR(1000),
            CREATED_AT TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_UTCTIMESTAMP,
            FOREIGN KEY (EMB_REF_ID) REFERENCES DOCUMENTS_EMBEDDING(ref_id)
        )
        """
        conn.execute_sql(create_sql)

        # Create indexes for fast lookups
        conn.execute_sql("CREATE INDEX IDX_TRIPLE_SUBJ ON TRIPLE_STORE (SUBJECT)")
        conn.execute_sql("CREATE INDEX IDX_TRIPLE_PRED ON TRIPLE_STORE (PREDICATE)")
        conn.execute_sql("CREATE INDEX IDX_TRIPLE_OBJ ON TRIPLE_STORE (OBJECT)")
        conn.execute_sql("CREATE INDEX IDX_TRIPLE_REF ON TRIPLE_STORE (EMB_REF_ID, CHUNK_INDEX)")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"error ensuring triple store: {e}")

Understanding the Schema

Column Type Purpose

IDBIGINT (auto)Unique triplet identifier
EMB_REF_IDNVARCHAR(36)Links to the embedding table — this is the bridge between vectors and graph
CHUNK_INDEXINTEGERWhich chunk within a document this triplet came from
SUBJECTNVARCHAR(500)The subject entity
PREDICATENVARCHAR(200)The relationship
OBJECTNVARCHAR(1000)The object entity
CREATED_ATTIMESTAMPWhen the triplet was created

The EMB_REF_ID foreign key is the critical design decision. It links every triplet back to its source embedding in the DOCUMENTS_EMBEDDING table. This means: - Given a vector search result, we can find its associated triplets - Given a triplet, we can find the original text chunk - The two systems (vector + graph) are tightly integrated

Indexes for Performance

i created four indexes for fast querying: - IDX_TRIPLE_SUBJ — look up triplets by subject - IDX_TRIPLE_PRED — look up triplets by predicate - IDX_TRIPLE_OBJ — look up triplets by object - IDX_TRIPLE_REF — look up triplets by source embedding (composite index)

The Embedding Table (for reference)

The companion DOCUMENTS_EMBEDDING table stores the vector embeddings:

CREATE COLUMN TABLE DOCUMENTS_EMBEDDING (
    id INTEGER GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
    document_text NVARCHAR(5000),
    embedding REAL_VECTOR(3072),
    chunk_metadata NVARCHAR(1000),
    ref_id NVARCHAR(36) UNIQUE NOT NULL
)

REAL_VECTOR(3072) is SAP HANA’s native vector type — it stores 3072-dimensional embeddings (from OpenAI’s text-embedding-3-large model) and supports cosine similarity searches natively.

Inserting Triplets

def insert_triplets(triplets_rows: list):
    """
    Insert triplets with their metadata
    triplets_rows: list of (ref_id, chunk_index, subject, predicate, object) tuples
    """
    ensure_triple_store()
    conn = get_hana_db()

    if not triplets_rows:
        return False

    sql = """
        INSERT INTO TRIPLE_STORE (EMB_REF_ID, CHUNK_INDEX, SUBJECT, PREDICATE, OBJECT)
        VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
    """
    try:
        cur = conn.connection.cursor()
        cur.executemany(sql, triplets_rows)
        conn.connection.commit()
        cur.close()
        return True
    except Exception as e:
        return False

We use executemany for batch insertion — inserting hundreds of triplets in a single database round-trip instead of one-by-one.

7. The Complete Ingestion Pipeline

Let’s see how everything comes together in our document_processing_service.py. This is the end-to-end flow: from a raw document URL to stored embeddings AND triplets.

async def process_and_embed_file_from_url(file_url: str):
    """
    Download file, extract text, create embeddings and triplets,
    store everything in HANA
    """
    # Step 1: Download and extract text
    text_content = process_file_from_url(file_url)

    # Step 2: Split into overlapping chunks
    chunks = split_text_into_chunks(text_content)

    # Step 3: Preprocess chunks
    preprocessed_chunks = preprocess_text_chunks(chunks)

    # Step 4: Generate unique reference IDs (linking embeddings ↔ triplets)
    ref_ids = [str(uuid.uuid4()) for _ in preprocessed_chunks]

    # Step 5: Create embeddings for all chunks (concurrent)
    embeddings = await embedding_service.get_embeddings_batch(preprocessed_chunks, max_workers=5)

    # Step 6: Extract triplets from all chunks (multi-agent pipeline)
    triplets_per_chunk = await convert_corpus_to_triplets_async(preprocessed_chunks)

    # Step 7: Insert embeddings FIRST (ref_ids must exist for FK constraint)
    rows = []
    for i, (chunk, embedding_vector) in enumerate(zip(preprocessed_chunks, embeddings)):
        metadata_json = json.dumps({
            "chunk_index": i,
            "chunk_size": len(chunk),
            "source_url": file_url,
            "ref_id": ref_ids[i],
        })
        embedding_string = str(embedding_vector)
        rows.append((chunk, embedding_string, metadata_json, ref_ids[i]))

    batch_insertion_embedding(rows=rows)

    # Step 8: Insert triplets (linked via ref_ids)
    triplets_rows = []
    for chunk_index, (ref_id, chunk_triplets) in enumerate(zip(ref_ids, triplets_per_chunk)):
        for t in chunk_triplets:
            if isinstance(t, dict):
                head, relation, tail = t.get("subject"), t.get("predicate"), t.get("object")
            else:
                head, relation, tail = t
            triplets_rows.append((ref_id, chunk_index, head, relation, tail))

    if triplets_rows:
        insert_triplets(triplets_rows)

The Text Chunking Strategy

def split_text_into_chunks(text: str, chunk_size: int = 1000, overlap: int = 200):
    """Split text into overlapping chunks for better embedding"""
    chunks = []
    start = 0
    while start < len(text):
        end = start + chunk_size
        chunk = text[start:end]
        chunks.append(chunk)
        start = end - overlap
    return chunks

i used overlapping chunks (200 characters overlap) so that facts spanning chunk boundaries aren’t lost. Each chunk is 1000 characters with a 200-character overlap with the next chunk.

The API Endpoint

This entire pipeline is exposed via a FastAPI endpoint in genai_hub.py:

@genai_router.post("/create-store-embedding", response_model=EmbeddingKGResponse)
async def create_and_store_embedding(
    request: EmbeddingKGRequest,
    background_tasks: BackgroundTasks
) -> EmbeddingKGResponse:
    """Create and store embeddings using file URL"""
    # Processing in background (non-blocking)
    background_tasks.add_task(process_and_embed_file_from_url, request.file_url)

    return EmbeddingKGResponse(
        success=True,
        message=f"Successfully processed and embedded document from {request.file_url}"
    )

The processing runs as a background task — the API responds immediately while the heavy work (embedding + triplet extraction) happens asynchronously.

8. Querying the Knowledge Graph

Now for the exciting part — retrieving knowledge from our graph. i have two query strategies in knowledge_graph_service.py.

Strategy 1: Chunk-Based Retrieval

Given a set of embedding reference IDs (from vector search results), fetch all associated triplets:

def get_triplets_by_chunks(ref_ids: list, query: str):
    """Getting triplets from specific chunks + optional query expansion"""
    conn = get_hana_db()

    ref_placeholders = ",".join(f"'{ref}'" for ref in ref_ids)
    base_sql = f"""
    SELECT SUBJECT, PREDICATE, OBJECT
    FROM TRIPLE_STORE
    WHERE EMB_REF_ID IN ({ref_placeholders})
    ORDER BY CHUNK_INDEX, ID
    LIMIT 200
    """

    triplets = []
    try:
        df = conn.sql(base_sql).collect()
        for _, row in df.iterrows():
            triplets.append((row["SUBJECT"], row["PREDICATE"], row["OBJECT"]))
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"An error occurred: {e}")

    # Optional: expand with query-related triplets
    if query and triplets:
        expanded = search_related_triplets(
            query, existing_entities=[t[0] for t in triplets[:10]]
        )
        triplets.extend(expanded)

    return triplets

This function does two things: 1. Direct retrieval — gets all triplets linked to the vector search results 2. Graph expansion — discovers additional related triplets based on entities found

Strategy 2: Keyword + Entity Expansion

The search_related_triplets function implements a two-phase search:

def search_related_triplets(query: str, existing_entities: list, limit: int = 50):
    """Searching triplets by keyword + entity expansion"""
    conn = get_hana_db()
    triplets = []

    # Phase 1: Keyword search in triplets
    query_clean = query.replace("'", "''")
    keyword_sql = f"""
    SELECT SUBJECT, PREDICATE, OBJECT
    FROM TRIPLE_STORE
    WHERE SUBJECT LIKE '%{query_clean}%'
    OR OBJECT LIKE '%{query_clean}%'
    OR PREDICATE LIKE '%{query_clean}%'
    LIMIT {limit}
    """
    try:
        df = conn.sql(keyword_sql).collect()
        for _, row in df.iterrows():
            triplets.append((row["SUBJECT"], row["PREDICATE"], row["OBJECT"]))
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"error in keyword triplet search: {e}")

    # Phase 2: Entity expansion
    if existing_entities:
        entities_clean = [e.replace("'", "''") for e in existing_entities[:5]]
        entities_placeholders = "','".join(entities_clean)
        expansion_sql = f"""
        SELECT SUBJECT, PREDICATE, OBJECT
        FROM TRIPLE_STORE
        WHERE SUBJECT IN ('{entities_placeholders}')
        OR OBJECT IN ('{entities_placeholders}')
        LIMIT {limit}
        """
        try:
            df = conn.sql(expansion_sql).collect()
            for _, row in df.iterrows():
                triplets.append((row["SUBJECT"], row["PREDICATE"], row["OBJECT"]))
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"error in entity expansion triplet search: {e}")

    return triplets

Phase 1 (Keyword Search): Finds triplets where the query terms appear anywhere in subject, predicate, or object. This catches direct mentions.

Phase 2 (Entity Expansion): Takes entities discovered in Phase 1 and finds ALL triplets mentioning those entities. This is the “graph traversal” — following connections one hop outward.

This is how we achieve multi-hop reasoning: Vector search finds relevant chunks → chunk triplets reveal entities → entity expansion reveals connected facts from other chunks.

9. Hybrid Retrieval: Vector + Graph

The ContextService in context_service.py is where vector search and knowledge graph come together:

class ContextService:
    """Context service with cache for RAG Pipeline"""

    async def hybrid_search_context(self, query: str, top_k: int = 5, expand_graph: bool = True) -> str:
        """
        Hybrid retrieval: vector similarity + graph retrieval
        Returns combined textual context for RAG
        """
        # Step 1: Generate query embedding
        query_embedding = await embedding_service.get_embedding(query)

        # Step 2: Vector similarity search
        vector_results = await search_similiar_documents(query_embedding, top_k=top_k)

        if vector_results is None or vector_results.empty:
            return ""

        context_parts = []
        ref_ids = []

        # Step 3: Extract results and collect ref_ids
        for _, row in vector_results.iterrows():
            ref_id = row.get("REF_ID") or row.get("ref_id")
            chunk_text = row.get("DOCUMENT_TEXT", '')
            similarity = row.get("SIMILARITY", 0)

            context_parts.append(f"Chunk (similarity: {similarity:.4f}): {chunk_text}")
            if ref_id:
                ref_ids.append(ref_id)

        # Step 4: Graph expansion using ref_ids from vector results
        if expand_graph and ref_ids:
            graph_context = get_triplets_by_chunks(ref_ids, query)
            if graph_context:
                context_parts.append(f"\nRelated Facts: {graph_context}")

        return "\n\n".join(context_parts)

The Hybrid Flow Visualized

User Query: "Who leads Project Phoenix?"
        │
        ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  1. Embed Query          │  → [0.023, -0.156, 0.891, ...]
└────────────┬────────────┘
             │
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  2. Vector Search        │  → Top 5 similar chunks
│  (COSINE_SIMILARITY)     │     with ref_ids
└────────────┬────────────┘
             │
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  3. Graph Expansion                      │
│  - Fetch triplets for those ref_ids      │
│  - Keyword search for "Project Phoenix"  │
│  - Entity expansion for found entities   │
└────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
             │
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  4. Combine Context      │
│  - Chunk texts           │  → "Chunk (similarity: 0.92): ..."
│  - Related facts         │  → "Related Facts: [(John Smith, leads, Project Phoenix), ...]"
└────────────┬────────────┘
             │
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  5. Send to LLM          │  → Final answer with full context
└─────────────────────────┘

10. The Full RAG Pipeline

Everything comes together in the /chat endpoint in RAG_pipeline.py:

@rag_pipeline_router.post("/chat", response_model=RAGChatResponse)
async def rag_chat(request: RAGChatRequest) -> RAGChatResponse:
    """Complete RAG pipeline: Retrieve + Augment + Generate"""
    try:
        # Check cache first
        cache_key = context_service.cache.make_key(
            "rag_response",
            _make_query_hash(request.query, request.temperature, request.max_tokens),
        )
        cached_response = await context_service.cache.get(cache_key)
        if cached_response:
            cached_response["from_cache"] = True
            return RAGChatResponse(**cached_response)

        # Hybrid retrieval (vector + graph)
        context = await context_service.hybrid_search_context(
            request.query, top_k=request.k, expand_graph=True
        )

        if context == "":
            return RAGChatResponse(
                success=True, query=request.query,
                answer="I couldn't find any relevant documents to answer your question.",
            )

        # Generate answer with augmented context
        answer = await generate_rag_response(
            request.query, context, request.temperature, request.max_tokens
        )

        response_data = {
            "success": True, "query": request.query,
            "answer": answer, "context_used": context, "from_cache": False
        }

        # Cache for 1 hour
        await context_service.cache.set(cache_key, response_data, ttl=3600)
        return RAGChatResponse(**response_data)
    except Exception as e:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail={"success": False, "error": str(e)})

The prompt sent to the LLM for generation:

async def generate_rag_response(query, context, temperature=0.1, max_tokens=500):
    prompt = f"""
    Based on the following context, answer the question:

    Context:
    {context}

    Question: {query}

    Answer based only on the context provided:
    """
    response = await llm_service.get_llm_response_async(prompt=prompt)
    return response

The context now contains both similar text chunks (from vectors) and structured facts (from the knowledge graph). The LLM gets the richest possible context to generate its answer.

11. Exposing via MCP for AI Agents

i also expose our hybrid RAG as an MCP (Model Context Protocol) tool in mcp/server.py, so AI agents can use it directly:

from fastmcp import FastMCP

mcp = FastMCP("AI-RAG-Service")

@mcp.tool()
async def rag_chat(query: str, top_k: int = 3, temperature: float = 0.1) -> str:
    """Chat with RAG pipeline - retrieves context and generates answer"""
    # Get context using hybrid search (vector + knowledge graph)
    context = await context_service.hybrid_search_context(
        query, top_k=top_k, expand_graph=True
    )
    if not context:
        return "I couldn't find any relevant documents to answer your question."

    prompt = f"""Based on the following context, answer the question:
    Context: {context}
    Question: {query}
    Answer based only on the context provided:"""

    answer = await llm_service.get_llm_response_async(prompt=prompt)
    return answer

This means any MCP-compatible agent (like those built with LangChain, LangGraph, or Claude) can call our rag_chat tool and get knowledge-graph-augmented answers automatically.

12. Architecture Summary

Here’s the complete system architecture:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        INGESTION PIPELINE                          │
│                                                                     │
│  Document URL → Download → Text Extraction → Chunking (1000/200)   │
│       │                                                             │
│       ├──→ Embedding Service ──→ DOCUMENTS_EMBEDDING table          │
│       │    (text-embedding-3-large, 3072 dims)                      │
│       │                                                             │
│       └──→ Multi-Agent Triplet Pipeline ──→ TRIPLE_STORE table      │
│            (Analyzer → Cleaner → Validator → Aggregator)            │
│                                                                     │
│  Tables linked by ref_id (UUID) ←─ Foreign Key relationship        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        RETRIEVAL PIPELINE                           │
│                                                                     │
│  User Query → Embed Query → Cosine Similarity Search (HANA Vector) │
│       │                                                             │
│       ├──→ Top-K similar chunks (text + ref_ids)                    │
│       │                                                             │
│       └──→ Graph Expansion (ref_id triplets + keyword + entities)   │
│                                                                     │
│  Combined Context → LLM Prompt → Generated Answer                   │
│                                                                     │
│  Exposed via: FastAPI REST + MCP Tools for AI Agents                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

13. Conclusion

Knowledge Graphs are not just an academic concept — they’re a practical, powerful enhancement to any enterprise RAG system. By combining vector embeddings with structured knowledge triplets stored in SAP HANA’s Triple Store, i achieved:

  1. Better answer quality — The LLM receives both semantically similar text AND structured facts
  2. Multi-hop reasoning — Entity expansion allows discovering connected facts across different documents
  3. Production-grade reliability — Multi-agent pipeline with retries, validation, and graceful degradation
  4. Tight integration — Foreign key linking between embeddings and triplets enables seamless hybrid retrieval
  5. Agent-ready — MCP tools let any AI agent leverage the knowledge graph instantly

Key Takeaways for Your Team

  • Start with LLM-based triplet extraction — it produces far better triplets than rule-based NLP
  • Always validate — the multi-agent pipeline (analyze → clean → validate → aggregate) catches errors early
  • Link your stores — the ref_id foreign key between embeddings and triplets is what makes hybrid retrieval possible
  • Index aggressively — Subject, Predicate, Object, and composite indexes make graph queries fast
  • Expand the graph — entity expansion is where the real magic happens, discovering connections the vector search alone would miss

The future of enterprise AI isn’t just “more data”, it’s more connected data. Knowledge Graphs give your AI agents not just context, but direction.

Have questions or want to discuss further? Reach out to me

entry.title: Request for Guidance on Production Staging Configuration in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello Team,

Requirement:
In our process, production is executed in the IM storage location, while the raw materials are stored at the warehouse level (EWM‑managed). Once a production order is released, the picking process must be initiated so that the required stock can be picked from the warehouse and moved to the IM production storage location.

Expected Process Flow:
After the production order is released, the system should trigger the picking process. Once picking is completed, the system should automatically create the corresponding delivery document or warehouse task. This will enable the warehouse operator to print the pick slip, locate the materials in the warehouse, and pick the required quantities.

I am currently following the 5HO process, but at the step “Stage Material for Production Order Level”, the system is not retrieving the production order number.

I kindly request guidance on the configuration steps required in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to support this process. Please share the relevant configuration details or documentation.

Thank you,
Kiran

entry.title: SAP SAC Best Practice for using reference articles to copy data to new articles
entry.content:
Hello,

I have the following use case and I am bit struggling with what the best practice in SAC is to solve it.

Let’s assume that I have a small planning model with the following dimensions:

  • Date
  • Article
  • Customer
  • Version

And the following measures:

  • Sales (entered in SAC)
  • Price (loaded from a source system)
  • Turnover (calculated with Sales * Price)

During a forecast planning cycle it could happen that sales for a new article is planned where no price was uploaded. Therefore, turnover could not be calculated.

I would like to create a report that gives all the articles with no prices. Here, the planner should be able to define a reference article from which the price can be copied.

Is there a best practice for such a requirement? I have searched through the community but could not find a solution that fits.

Thanks and best regards!

Hi @saikhancs35

IF RESULTLOOKUP([d/Version]=%Source_Version%)!= NULL  THEN

entry.title: Re: Data Integration in ABAP Cloud: Data Exposure using SQL services
entry.content:
Hello @akuller 

for this purpose, we  provide the "Writable External Entities" which allow modifying data in external databases using ABAP SQL operations like INSERT, UPDATE, MODIFY, and DELETE, enabling write access in data integration scenarios. This is done via a service connection to separate remote and local transactions. For more details, see Writable External Entities. 

in my next post I'll introduce the concept External Entities with examples. 

Best regards

Safa

entry.title: Re: if any method we can avoid to using PMP0 as the actua...
entry.content:
You cannot change the fact that actual costs are posted to a G/L account and that is the essence of PMP0.

What you can influence is that

  • which G/L account is used (via account determination customizing).
  • whether the posting goes to a WBS, cost center, or internal order (via account assignment in the PO/service confirmation).
  • whether secondary cost elements are used for internal allocations (but PMP0 is always primary).

Also, to my knowledge, there is no BAPI to “change” PMP0 behavior. PMP0 is the standard primary cost posting. If you need different accounting treatment, you must adjust account determination customizing or post adjustments via FI/CO BAPIs.

entry.title: Re: Foreign exchange gain/loss incorrect
entry.content:
Good day @Mel3 

Thank you for your question.

In Public Cloud, it is possible to see a clearing journal showing an absolute exchange rate like 1.15808, while the realised FX gain or loss like it reflects an older effective rate like 1.11607. This usually happens because the realised gain or loss is calculated from the difference between:

  1. The local currency amount already stored on the open item (from the original invoice posting, or from the last valuation run), and
  2. The local currency amount at clearing based on the clearing date rate.

The following reference lists multiple causes for large or unexpected exchange differences during clearing, including date logic, rate type logic and prior valuation behaviour.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/fiaccounting/3361880873.html?locale=en-US

Here is a quick check to carry out:

  1. In your system, review the invoice journal entry values in your Manage Journal Entries
  2. In Schedule General Ledger Jobs, check whether Foreign Currency Valuation ran before clearing
  3. In Currency Exchange Rates app, check the exchange rates available in the system for both dates

 

Question: How and why would it have an absolute exchange rate in the journal but then use an older rate as the profit/loss?

Answer: The reason why the clearing document can show 1.15808 but the gain or loss look like 1.11607, is the clearing document header rate can be correct for the clearing date, but the FX difference is driven by the difference between the stored local currency valuation of the open item and the clearing valuation. This means the “older effective rate” is typically coming from one of these:

  • The invoice was posted earlier using that older rate and that local currency amount became the baseline for the open item.
  • A validation run posted unrealised FD differences earlier and clearing later reverses or offsets that valuation logic, which can make the resulting realised difference look unexpected. See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/0982d941088149fd9ef65f83ad749d70.html?locale=en-US

  • Different translation date logic is applied, for example a configuration where document date is used as translation date for exchange rate determination, which can pull an older rate even if you posted earlier. See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/ee9ee0ca4c3942068ea584d2f929b5b1/5965c377d1364e70836beea6fbf6276b.html?locale=en-US

  • Different exchange rate determination rules apply, so one step uses a different rate selection rule than you expect. See the following reference for more details. See the following reference for more details. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b8c08e0197454541a11f8d46ef1ab96e/b6d1b0f38c974cd0ac600d1b8111d80f.html?locale=en-US

Recommended best practice for low volume foreign payments, if you only update rates monthly, you increase the risk that:

  • Invoices posted with older document dates.
  • Late clearing activity or valuation runs.

Will reference older stored values and create large, realised FX differences.

So, the best practice is to maintain rates for the dates you post and clear or use a controlled replication approach.

See the following reference for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/8c01d153c9684608e10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP SAC Best Practice for using reference articles to...
entry.content:
Hi @SDMS90 

In my view, such scenario can be can fulfilled by using a mix of validation reporting + planner driven correction + Data Action + minimal scripting.

Steps :

  • Create a calculated measure with the condition (IF([Price] = 0 AND [Sales] > 0, 1, 0)) and create a new table(validation report) using the measure.
  • Create a input control with all the referenced articles.
  • Create a data action with parameter reference article and missing article as parameter and add a copy step or advanced data step to copy the data from reference article to missing article. 
  • Create a publish button step on the main sales entry form. We will using the custom publish button to validate and publish prices.
  • You can hide the main publish button for the story using the view time setting.
  • Hide the objects created in Step a , b and c.

Proposed workflow

  • Planner enters Sales in the planning table.
  • Planner clicks the custom Publish button.
  • The system checks the validation table (calculated measure: IF([Price] = 0 AND [Sales] > 0, 1, 0)) to identify records with missing prices.
  • If missing prices exist, the Reference Article Input Control and Data Action button become visible.
  • The planner selects a reference article and runs the Data Action to copy the price to the article with the missing price.
  • After correcting the missing prices, the planner clicks Publish again.
  • This process continues until no records appear in the validation table, ensuring all required prices are maintained before final publish.

You can use scripts to make it more interactive with the users and achieve the desired workflow.If the number of article with missing prices is high you can maintain the reference article/reference article group attribute to copy the prices automatically also.

This is one of the proposed workflow which comes to my mind and there are also alternative ways to achieved the requirement.

Regards

Shivam

entry.title: Re: FM/BAPI to extract the stock report of MB52 t-code
entry.content:
Have seen using AMDP to fetch the logic is the fastes one than the FM as it can be used for printing Forms.
Consider the key fields Material, Plant, Sloc, Batch, special stock, Special stock number (Vendor, Customer, Sales order/item, Project)
Batch Stocks  MCHB
Vendor ConsignmentKMKOL/MARD
Returnable Transport Pkg VendorMMKOL
Sales Order StockEMSKA
Project StockQMSPR/MSSQ
Subcontracting vendorOMSLB/MSSL
Pipeline MaterialPMARD
Customer ConsignmentWMSKU
RTP customerVMSKU

1. Consignment Stock (Vendor) – Indicator 'K'
  • Business: Materials provided by a vendor are stored on your premises. You do not own the stock and pay the vendor only when it is consumed or removed from the warehouse for production/sale.
  • Process: Goods Receipt is posted via 101K; liability is settled via MRKO.
  • Usage: Vendor-managed inventory, reducing upfront procurement costs. 
2. Sales Order Stock – Indicator 'E'
  • Business: Stock is manufactured or procured specifically for a specific customer sales order. It is reserved for this order and cannot be used for any other purpose.
  • Usage: Make-to-order manufacturing, customized products. 
3. Project Stock – Indicator 'Q'
  • Business: Material is procured or manufactured specifically for a WBS (Work Breakdown Structure) element in a project. This stock is linked to project accounting and cannot be used for other purposes.
  • Usage: Engineering, Construction, Defense industries.
4. Subcontracting Stock – Indicator 'O'
  • Business: Raw materials or components are sent to a vendor (subcontractor) for processing or assembly. The material remains under your company's ownership while at the subcontractor's site.
  • Usage: Outsourcing production steps, component tracking (MIGO 541/542). 
5. Returnable Transport Packaging (RTP) – Indicator 'M' 
  • Business: Reusable packaging materials (pallets, containers) belonging to a vendor are stored at your site. They must be returned to the vendor after use.
  • Usage: Logistics and material handling between suppliers and customers. 
6. Pipeline Material – Indicator 'P'
  • Business: Materials that enter the production process directly from a pipeline, cable, or other type of conduit (e.g., water, gas, electricity).
  • Usage: Energy consumption, fluid raw materials. 
7. Customer Consignment Stock – Indicator 'W'
  • Business: Stock belonging to your company is stored at a customer's location. It remains your inventory until the customer consumes it.
  • Usage: Vendor-managed inventory (VMI) at customer site. 
8. Returnable Packaging with Customer – Indicator 'V' 
  • Business: Your company sends returnable packaging (e.g., pallets, containers) to a customer, which must be returned.
  • Usage: Tracking reusable transport assets sent out to customers

entry.title: Re: How to implement a checker /maker workflow(approval workflow ) in sap hcm tcode pa40 for employ
entry.content: Hi SurafMulu, For PA40 actions, the usual approach is to set up a maker/checker workflow using SAP HCM Workflow or HR Renewal & Approval frameworks. Typically, you define: Initiator (Maker) – the person who creates or requests the employee action. Approver (Checker) – the person(s) who need to approve the action before it’s finalized. Workflow rules – conditions based on employee data, action type, or organizational unit. Notifications – emails/tasks triggered for the checker to approve. You can implement this either via standard SAP HR workflows linked to PA40 actions or by customizing workflow templates in SWDD. It’s important to test with sample employee actions to ensure approvals trigger correctly. The key is planning your approval hierarchy first, then mapping it to the workflow rules in SAP.

entry.title: Re: FI: GR/IR account openitem clearing
entry.content:
Good day @nithin_pranesh 

Thank you for your question.

The GR/IR clearing accounts in Public Cloud are not meant to be cleared using the generic “Clear G/L Accounts-Manual Clearing” app. This is why your GR/IR open items do not appear there, even though they exist in the ledger.

See the following reference for more insights

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2935422

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/19712fd7426a4baca53b95fac7301a39.html?locale=en-US

I recommend you use the GR/IR specific reconciliation and clearing process. First, use “Reconciled GR/IR Accounts” to analyse the unmatched items. Then use GR/IR automatic clearing job template and execute the GR/IR automatic clearing via Schedule General Ledger Jobs.

See the following reference for more details

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3147360

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/ee9ee0ca4c3942068ea584d2f929b5b1/a1dd39b8e23f4b9a80e7156385d7d0e9.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/9a5c02d9359b4d9e888396409098001c.html?locale=en-US

If the opening balance upload created open items that do not follow GR/IR grouping logic, they may not be matchable with a manually posted invoice line on GR/IR. In that case, the corrective action is to reconciled through the GR/IR apps and going forward, adjust the opening balance approach to GR/IR compatible method.

This reference provides some insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/17f3a45189524e78b4a80bf51ff2b741.html?locale=en-US

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3147360

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris
